entries.title: WLAN AutoConfig successfully connected to a wireless network
entries.summary: The WLAN AutoConfig service successfully associated and authenticated to a wireless network.
entries.details: Generated when Windows successfully connects to a wireless network including completion of 802.11 association and authentication (WPA2/WPA3/open). Unexpected connections to unfamiliar SSIDs, ad-hoc networks, or connections outside business hours on managed endpoints are notable. Rogue access points mimicking a corporate SSID (evil-twin attacks) generate a legitimate 8001 from the victim's perspective; the BSSID (recorded in the associated 11001 event) is the distinguishing field. Ad-hoc (peer-to-peer) connections are inherently untrusted.

prerequisites.description: The Microsoft-Windows-WLAN-AutoConfig/Operational log must be enabled. It is disabled by default on some configurations.

investigationPivots: SSID not matching any known corporate wireless network name is the primary pivot — compare against a maintained list of authorised SSIDs
BSSType = 'Independent' (ad-hoc/IBSS) should never appear on a managed endpoint and indicates peer-to-peer wireless activity
AuthenticationAlgorithm = 'Open' (no auth) on a corporate device is anomalous — rogue open APs are used for credential capture
Correlate InterfaceGuid with the physical adapter in Device Manager to confirm which NIC connected
Correlate timestamp with user logon events (Security EID 4624) to attribute the connection to a specific user session
commonFalsePositives: Normal daily wireless connections to authorised corporate or home SSIDs
Mobile hotspot connections used by travelling employees
Wireless adapter connecting to a guest network during a site visit

keyFields.name: SSID
keyFields.description: Network name (SSID) connected to; compare against authorised SSID list

keyFields.name: InterfaceGuid
keyFields.description: GUID of the wireless network adapter; links to the physical interface

keyFields.name: BSSType
keyFields.description: Infrastructure (normal AP) vs. Independent (ad-hoc/peer-to-peer); ad-hoc is high-risk

keyFields.name: AuthenticationAlgorithm
keyFields.description: Authentication method; Open (no auth) is highest risk on a managed device

keyFields.name: PHYType
keyFields.description: Wireless protocol type (802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax)

detectionRules.title: Wireless Connection to Unknown SSID
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-WLAN-AutoConfig"
| where EventID == 8001
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="SSID">' SSID '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="BSSType">' BSSType '</Data>' *
| where SSID !in ("Corp-WiFi", "CorpWLAN", "Guest-WiFi") // Replace with known-good SSIDs
   or BSSType == "Independent"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SSID, BSSType
detectionRules.notes: Replace the SSID list with your organisation's authorised wireless network names. Ad-hoc (Independent) connections should always alert.

detectionRules.title: Wireless Ad-Hoc Connection Detected
detectionRules.rule:
title: Wireless Ad-Hoc Network Connection
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: wlan-autoconfig
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 8001
        BSSType: 'Independent'
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Intentional peer-to-peer wireless use in lab environments
level: medium

entries.title: WLAN AutoConfig failed to connect to a wireless network
entries.summary: The WLAN AutoConfig service failed to associate or authenticate to a wireless network.
entries.details: Generated when a wireless connection attempt fails at the association or authentication phase. Repeated failures against known corporate SSIDs from a device may indicate a compromised or misconfigured credential. Failure against an unknown SSID may indicate an automated or attacker-driven probe. ReasonCode 0x0003 (authentication/security failure) on 802.1x networks indicates rejected EAP credentials. High volumes of 8002 without successful 8001 events from a single device may indicate a deauthentication or jamming attack preventing connection.

investigationPivots: ReasonCode 0x0003 on an 802.1x network indicates EAP credential rejection — cross-reference NPS log for the specific failure reason
High frequency of 8002 with no successful 8001 events suggests the device cannot reach the AP — possible deauthentication/jamming attack
Failure against an SSID the device has no profile for indicates a probing tool or misconfiguration
commonFalsePositives: Incorrect Wi-Fi password after a password rotation
Temporary AP unavailability causing transient failures
Expired or revoked machine certificate in 802.1x environments

keyFields.name: InterfaceGuid
keyFields.description: GUID of the wireless network adapter

keyFields.name: SSID
keyFields.description: Target network name; failure against an unknown SSID is higher risk

keyFields.name: ReasonCode
keyFields.description: Failure reason; 0x0003 = authentication failure (bad credential or rejected EAP)

entries.title: WLAN AutoConfig disconnected from a wireless network
entries.summary: The WLAN AutoConfig service disconnected from a previously connected wireless network.
entries.details: Generated when a wireless session ends. This event combined with the preceding 8001 event provides a wireless session timeline. DisconnectReason 0x0004 (AP sent deauthentication) or 0x0008 (AP sent disassociation) may indicate a deauthentication attack forcing a reconnect, which is a precursor to a capture-the-handshake or evil-twin attack. Unexpected disconnections from corporate SSIDs followed immediately by 8001 events to a different SSID are a strong indicator of an evil-twin attack in progress.

investigationPivots: DisconnectReason indicating AP-initiated deauthentication (0x0004 or 0x0008) followed by a new 8001 to a different SSID indicates a possible evil-twin attack
Pair with EID 8001 to calculate session duration — very short sessions followed by reconnection to a different SSID are anomalous
Correlate with NetworkProfile EID 10001 to confirm the network profile was also dropped
commonFalsePositives: Normal disconnection when a laptop moves out of range, enters a building, or is put to sleep
Network adapter driver updates causing reconnects
Roaming between corporate APs on the same SSID

keyFields.name: InterfaceGuid
keyFields.description: GUID of the wireless network adapter

keyFields.name: SSID
keyFields.description: Network disconnected from; pair with next 8001 event to detect SSID switching

keyFields.name: Reason
keyFields.description: Disconnect reason; AP-initiated deauthentication is a red flag for evil-twin attacks

entries.title: Wireless network association started
entries.summary: The wireless adapter began the 802.11 association process with an access point.
entries.details: Generated at the start of the 802.11 association process, before authentication completes. The BSSID field is the primary field for evil-twin detection because an evil-twin AP shares the target SSID but uses a different BSSID (MAC address). The presence of a 11000 without a subsequent 11001 or 11002 may indicate an aborted association attempt.

investigationPivots: BSSID (AP MAC address) not matching the known list of authorised corporate APs for the matching SSID is the primary evil-twin indicator
BSSID changes for the same SSID between connections indicate the client is connecting to different physical APs — verify this is expected roaming
Correlate with physical location data (badge access, VPN status) to determine whether wireless activity is expected at that time
commonFalsePositives: Normal roaming between authorised APs on the same SSID (BSSID changes are expected)
Replacement or addition of new corporate APs — BSSID list requires maintenance

keyFields.name: SSID
keyFields.description: Network name being associated to

keyFields.name: BSSID
keyFields.description: MAC address of the access point; compare against authorised AP MAC list to detect evil-twin APs

keyFields.name: PHYType
keyFields.description: Wireless protocol (802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax); unexpected downgrades may indicate a downgrade attack

keyFields.name: InterfaceGuid
keyFields.description: GUID of the wireless network adapter

entries.title: Wireless network association succeeded
entries.summary: The wireless adapter successfully associated with an access point.
entries.details: Generated when the 802.11 association process completes successfully. At this point the client is associated with the AP but full network connectivity depends on subsequent authentication (WPA2/WPA3 handshake or 802.1x). The cipher fields are security-critical: UnicastCipher = CCMP (AES) is expected for WPA2/WPA3, while TKIP indicates a downgrade to WPA1 which is vulnerable to attacks. AuthenticationAlgorithm = 'Open80211' on a non-guest network is anomalous.

investigationPivots: UnicastCipher = TKIP instead of CCMP indicates connection to an older or downgraded AP — investigate the AP configuration
AuthenticationAlgorithm = 'Open80211' on a managed corporate device connecting to a corporate SSID indicates an evil-twin AP or policy misconfiguration
BSSID from this event carries forward from EID 11000 — compare against authorised AP list
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate association events during normal wireless sessions

keyFields.name: SSID
keyFields.description: Network name that was associated to

keyFields.name: BSSID
keyFields.description: AP MAC address; correlate with authorised AP inventory

keyFields.name: AuthenticationAlgorithm
keyFields.description: Auth type; 'Open80211' on corporate SSID is anomalous

keyFields.name: UnicastCipher
keyFields.description: Encryption cipher; CCMP (AES) is expected; TKIP indicates WPA1 downgrade

keyFields.name: MulticastCipher
keyFields.description: Multicast/broadcast encryption cipher

entries.title: Wireless network association failed
entries.summary: The wireless adapter failed to associate with an access point.
entries.details: Generated when the 802.11 association process fails before authentication completes. This event fires before any credential exchange; failures here indicate a physical or protocol-layer rejection rather than a credential issue. Repeated association failures to the same BSSID may indicate AP-side filtering (MAC address filtering blocking the device) or a protocol mismatch. Repeated failures followed by a successful association to a different BSSID for the same SSID is characteristic of deauthentication attacks forcing a client to an attacker-controlled AP.

investigationPivots: Pattern of 11002 against one BSSID followed by 11000/11001 against a different BSSID for the same SSID indicates forced roaming — possible deauth attack
FailureReason indicating peer rejection may indicate MAC filtering or AP-side deny listing of the device
commonFalsePositives: Transient association failures during normal roaming between corporate APs
AP firmware update causing momentary service interruption

keyFields.name: SSID
keyFields.description: Network name the association was attempted against

keyFields.name: BSSID
keyFields.description: AP MAC address that rejected association; compare with subsequent successful BSSID

keyFields.name: FailureReason
keyFields.description: Reason for association failure; peer rejection vs. timeout vs. protocol mismatch
